title: Summarize-and-Forget
concept: Periodically compressing old turns into a summary while keeping recent turns verbatim, hybridizing both strategies.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: In Sliding Window History, what happens to turns that fall outside the recent 'window'?

options.text: They are dropped or summarized, keeping only the N most recent turns verbatim
They get translated into a different language
They are sent to a human moderator for review

exercises.explain: Sliding Window History keeps only the most recent N turns exactly as written, dropping (or lightly summarizing) everything older to keep the context small and manageable.

cards.title: Why keep EVERYTHING or NOTHING?
cards.body: What if you could remember the gist of an old conversation without storing every single word... but still recall the last few messages perfectly? That's the sweet spot this lesson unlocks.

cards.title: What is Summarize-and-Forget?
cards.body: It's a memory strategy where old conversation turns get periodically compressed into a short summary, while the most recent turns are kept word-for-word. This blends the two prior strategies: full context and sliding windows.

cards.title: Like laundry: fold the old, keep the fresh out
cards.body: Fresh clothes stay out ready to wear (recent turns, verbatim). Older clothes get folded and put away compactly (old turns, summarized) instead of thrown out or left in a messy pile.

cards.title: Tutoring Bot Example
cards.body: After 20 turns of a math tutoring session, turns 1-15 get squished into: 'Student struggled with fractions, now practicing decimals.' Turns 16-20 stay exactly as typed, so the bot remembers the last few exchanges precisely.

cards.title: Myth: Summarizing means losing important info
cards.body: Not true if done well! A good summary keeps key facts (names, decisions, preferences) while dropping filler. Recent turns stay verbatim for precision, so nothing critical gets lost -- it just gets compressed.

cards.title: Two Extremes vs. The Hybrid
cards.body: Full history keeps everything but grows huge. Sliding window stays small but forgets. Summarize-and-Forget blends both: compact AND informed.

left.text: Old turns just vanish -- gone forever, no trace left

right.text: Old turns shrink into a summary -- gist stays, detail fades

exercises.prompt: What is the main benefit of Summarize-and-Forget over a pure sliding window (which just drops old turns)?

options.text: It uses way more tokens than keeping the full history
It preserves the gist of older context instead of discarding it completely
It requires no extra processing at all
It guarantees the model never makes mistakes

exercises.explain: A pure sliding window just deletes old turns once they fall outside the window, losing that information forever. Summarize-and-Forget instead compresses old turns into a summary, so key details survive in a compact form rather than vanishing.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In Summarize-and-Forget, the summary of old turns is regenerated only once, at the very start of the conversation.
exercises.explain: False. The summarization happens periodically as the conversation grows -- older turns get folded into (or added to) the summary again and again, not just once at the start, since there's no summary needed yet at turn one.

exercises.sentence: In Summarize-and-Forget, old turns are compressed into a summary, but the most recent turns are kept verbatim.
exercises.explain: Verbatim means word-for-word, unchanged. Recent turns are kept verbatim so the model has precise, accurate detail about what just happened, while older turns are compressed since exact wording matters less over time.

exercises.prompt: Which of these summaries was most likely AI-generated to compress old conversation turns (concise, factual, third-person)?

options.text: omg this convo is SO long lol anyway idk what to say next hehe
User previously asked about refund policy, confirmed order #4521, and requested a callback.
asdkj order thing yes no maybe callback???

exercises.explain: Option B reads like a clean, structured, third-person summary focused on key facts (order number, request type) -- exactly what an AI would produce when compressing old turns. The others are too chaotic or informal to be a useful summary.

exercises.prompt: A support chatbot has been talking to a user for 40 turns about a complex refund issue. Using Summarize-and-Forget, what should the context sent to the model look like at turn 41?

options.text: All 40 turns pasted in full, verbatim, no changes
A short summary of turns 1-30 plus turns 31-40 kept verbatim
Only turn 40, with everything else deleted permanently
A summary of turns 31-40 plus turns 1-30 kept verbatim

exercises.explain: Summarize-and-Forget compresses the OLDER turns into a compact summary (so key facts like the refund reason aren't lost) while keeping the MOST RECENT turns exactly as written (so the model has precise, fresh detail for what's happening right now). Option B does exactly that: old stuff compressed, new stuff verbatim. Option A wastes tokens on old detail that isn't needed word-for-word. Option C loses important history entirely. Option D backwards compresses the wrong end of the conversation.
